data.title: European Equities Track Lower in Friday Trading; Apple, Meta May Face Lower EU Digital Markets Act Fines
data.description:
The European stock markets were tracking lower in Friday trading as The Stoxx Europe 600 fell 0.69%, Germany's DAX was down 0.79%, the FTSE 100 in London was unchanged, France's CAC 40 declined 0.78%, and the Swiss Market Index was off 0.06%.

The UK's Competition and Markets Authority is consulting on whether to accept commitments from five airlines to resolve competition concerns over their transatlantic cooperation, the regulator said Friday.

Meanwhile, retail sales volumes in the UK rose an estimated 1.0% in February, according to the Office for National Statistics. This is compared with a 1.4% increase in retail sales volume in January.

In France, the consumer price index in March rose 0.8% from a year earlier, according to provisional estimates from the Institute for Statistics and Economic Studies. The prices of services, particularly insurance and food, accelerated, were partially offset by declining prices of energy and manufactured goods. On a monthly basis, consumer prices increased an estimated 0.2%.

In Spain, the annual consumer price index rose 2.3% in March, seven-tenths of a percentage point lower than in February, according to the Spanish Statistical Office. Meanwhile, the annual rate of the leading underlying inflation indicator declined two-tenths of a percentage point to 2.0%.

And in corporate news, Apple and Meta Platforms could be charged with lower fines under the European Union's Digital Markets Act, or DMA, as the European Commission seeks to avoid potential backlash from US President Donald Trump, the Financial Times reported Friday, citing sources familiar with the matter. The European Commission declined MT Newswires' comment request, while Apple and Meta Platforms did not immediately respond to requests for statements.

Eli Lilly's early Alzheimer's drug, Kisunla, should not be granted marketing authorization, according to a recommendation by the European Medicines Agency's Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use. Eli Lilly did not immediately reply to a request for comment.

French biopharmaceutical company DBV Technologies late Thursday disclosed financing of up to $306.9 million to fund a peanut allergy patch for children aged four to seven years old. The financing to be received includes gross proceeds of $125.5 million upon closing and up to $181.4 million if all the warrants in the sale are exercised, the company developing the Viaskin peanut patch to treat pediatric peanut allergy said.

PayPal could be subjected to fees by the European Union as part of a tariff dispute with the US, Reuters reported Friday, citing Bernd Lange, head of the European Parliament's international trade committee. PayPal did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' request for comment.

data.title: OncoZenge Seals EU Commercialization Deal With Italy's Molteni Farmaceutici
data.description:
OncoZenge signed the binding deal that grants Molteni Farmaceutici the exclusive rights to commercialize its BupiZenge oral pain treatment in Europe.

The agreement includes milestone payments as well as a number of royalties, according to a Friday release.

The Swedish oral pain medicine company and the Italian pharmaceutical company entered a ​nonbinding agreement for the European distribution of BupiZenge in January and finalized the deal after completing all due diligence procedures.

data.title: REG - Leverage Shares PLC Leverage -3XShrtAli£ Leverage-3XShrtAlbb$ Leverage-3XShrtAlbbE - Result ETP Securityholder Meeting - -3x Alibaba
data.description:
RNS Number : 7653C Leverage Shares PLC 28 March 2025  

NOTICE TO ETP SECURITYHOLDERS OF RESULT OF ADJOURNED MEETING

To the holders of the outstanding

Leverage Shares -3x Short Alibaba (BABA) ETP Securities

ISIN: XS2472334585

(the "ETP Securityholders" and the "ETPs" respectively)

of Leverage Shares plc (the "Issuer")

NOTICE IS HEREBY GIVEN to the holders of the above ETPs that, at the adjourned meeting of the ETP Securityholders held at 2nd Floor, Block 5, Irish Life Centre, Abbey Street Lower, Dublin 1, Ireland at 2.30pm Dublin time on 28 March 2025 (the "Adjourned Meeting") the Extraordinary Resolution set out in Schedule 1 hereto was duly passed.

Yours sincerely

The Directors

Leverage Shares plc

Date: 28 March 2025 

SCHEDULE 1

EXTRAORDINARY RESOLUTION

"THAT this meeting of the holders of the Leverage Shares -3x Short Alibaba (BABA) ETP Securities of Leverage Shares plc currently outstanding (the "ETP Securityholders", the "ETP Securities" and the "Issuer" respectively) constituted by the master trust deed originally dated 5 December 2017 as most recently amended on 17 July 2024 and as supplemented by a supplemental trust deed dated 7 June 2022 (the "Trust Deed") made between, among others, the Issuer and Apex Corporate Trustees (UK) Limited (the "Trustee") as trustee for the ETP Securityholders hereby resolves by way of Extraordinary Resolution to:

1.         assent to the Consolidation and authorise and direct the Trustee to concur and agree to the Consolidation and authorise and direct the Trustee and the Issuer, where applicable, to execute the Consolidation Supplemental Trust Deed (the matters referred to above, the "Proposal");

2.         sanction every abrogation, modification, variation, compromise, waiver or arrangement in respect of the rights of the ETP Securityholders appertaining to the ETP Securities, whether or not such rights arise under the Trust Deed, involved in or resulting from or effected by the Proposal and its implementation or otherwise necessary or appropriate to carry out and give effect to the Proposal;

3.         waive any and all formalities described in and required by the Trust Deed, the ETP Securities, the Conditions and/or any other document relating to the ETP Securities in connection with the Proposal;

4.         authorise, direct, request and empower the Trustee and the Issuer to concur in the Proposal and, in order to give effect thereto and to implement the same, to execute the Consolidation Supplemental Trust Deed and to execute and do, all such other deeds, instruments, acts and things as may be necessary, expedient, desirable or appropriate to carry out and give effect to this Extraordinary Resolution and the implementation of the Proposal;

5.         discharge and exonerate the Trustee and the Issuer from all and any liability for which they may have become or may become responsible under the Trust Deed or the ETP Securities in respect of any act or omission in connection with the Proposal, its implementation or this Extraordinary Resolution and its implementation;

6.         irrevocably and unconditionally waive any claim that we may have against the Trustee as a result of anything done or omitted to be done by the Trustee in good faith in connection with this (i) Extraordinary Resolution, (ii) the Consolidation, (iii) the Consolidation Supplemental Trust Deed and/or (iv) the Proposal;

7.         indemnify the Trustee, on demand, against any cost, loss or liability incurred in connection with (i) any act (or omission to act) or step implementing this Extraordinary Resolution, (ii) the Consolidation, (iii) the Consolidation Supplemental Trust Deed and or (iv) the Proposal; unless such cost, loss or liability has been caused by the Trustee's fraud, gross negligence or wilful default; and

8.         acknowledge that capitalised terms used in this Extraordinary Resolution have the same meanings as those defined in the Notice of Adjourned Meeting and/ or the Trust Deed (including the Conditions of the ETP Securities), unless otherwise defined herein or unless the context otherwise requires."

This information is provided by RNS, the news service of the London Stock Exchange. RNS is approved by the Financial Conduct Authority to act as a Primary Information Provider in the United Kingdom. data.title: REG - Schroder AsiaPacific - Transaction in Own Shares
data.description:
RNS Number : 7657C Schroder AsiaPacific Fund PLC 28 March 2025  

Transaction in own shares

Schroder AsiaPacific Fund plc (the "Company") announces that on Friday 28 March 2025 it purchased 35,000 of its ordinary shares at a price of 529.25 pence per share, to be cancelled.

Following this purchase, the Company's issued share capital consists of 139,135,216 ordinary shares of 10p each, the total number of shares in treasury is 0 and the total number of voting rights in the Company is 139,135,216.

The above figure may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the Company under the Financial Conduct Authority's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

Enquiries:

Schroder Investment Management Limited

Tel: 020 7658 6000

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1116 ET - The S&P 500 still remains expensive even after declining from its peak, say analysts at BofA Securities in a research note, as they handicap the investing landscape in the U.S. and Europe. According to the analysts, the bear case for US equities includes the capex arms race between hyperscalers increasing margin risk, among other things, and growing policy uncertainty amid higher government debt. But there are still structural advantages in the U.S., the analysts say, including clean corporate--and most consumer--balance sheets; high corporate transparency; USD reserve status; energy independence; looser labor laws, and U.S. technology and innovation still the world's leader. European equities, meanwhile, have a bull case amid stimulus from the German government and defense spending by the European Union. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0950 ET — Galaxy Digital Holdings expects to make billions on an agreement with CoreWeave, the startup that rents out access to Nvidia chips. The digital asset and blockchain company entered into a 15-year lease agreement with CoreWeave, which makes its public debut later Friday, in which it will provide 133 megawatts of data center capacity to host and support CoreWeave's artificial intelligence and high-performance computing efforts. Over the life of the agreement, Galaxy expects to generate about $4.5 billion in revenue. Benchmark's Mark Palmer in a research note says the estimated revenue from the deal likely reflects CoreWeave's commitment to fund all the capital investments required to upgrade its data centers, among other things. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0829 ET - A Telus sale of a minority interest in its wireless tower portfolio could be a good way to help deleverage the company. While the decision isn't final, Desjardins' Jerome Dubreuil says that there shouldn't be any regulatory issues, and that it shouldn't interfere with its network-sharing agreement with BCE. "Infrastructure divestitures have the potential to help deleveraging and to unlock value," Dubreuil says. He notes that management indicated that all of the potential proceeds would go toward debt repayment.(adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0812 ET - Braze's latest results didn't appear to show evidence of deterioration despite concerns over the health of the consumer, nor did it flag a material change in the demand environment, say UBS' Taylor McGinnis and Jeff Hickey in a research note. The customer-engagement platform logged higher revenue in F4Q, while its guidance for FY26 met expectations, the analysts say. They think that while Braze isn't commenting on the timing of a bottom, it could happen as early as 1Q. "We continue to like the set-up for Braze given company-specific tailwinds related to improving execution, lapping of tough Covid renewals and the incumbent placement opportunity," say the analysts. Braze jumps 12% premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0705 ET - Canada's telcos may be more resilient side if tariffs escalate, and could even navigate the worst of a recession if it were to come to that. TD Cowen's Vince Valentini says in a report that telecom services are to consumers "second in importance only to food, shelter, electricity." Meaning that wireless service and internet will be a top priority to maintain, even in an economic downturn. The analyst acknowledges that telcos aren't immune, however. Revenue could be slightly weighed at the margin such as smaller package sizes for cable, lower roaming revenue due to less travel abroad, and perhaps more aggressive shopping for a better deal on wireless plans. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0001 ET - Taiwan could face smaller tariff hikes due to its investments in the U.S. and key tech exports, says Lynn Song, ING's Greater China chief economist, in a note. Taiwan has been under scrutiny due to its rising trade surplus with the U.S. over the past years, which amounted to $64.9 billion in 2024. However, Taiwan's key exports of semiconductors and computer products to the U.S. have limited replacement alternatives, raising the probability of being exempted, the economist says. Also, TSMC's expanding investment in the U.S. "may have also brought Taiwan enough goodwill to soften or avert tariffs," he says. As a result, Taiwan could be one of the more likely economies to benefit from a tariff reprieve, or at least smaller tariff hikes, he adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

1520 ET - Fiber operations are a big opportunity for telecommunications companies to capitalize on the rise of data-center construction to power artificial intelligence, Citizens analyst Greg Miller tells WSJ. Data centers are increasingly being built in remote areas like North Dakota and Alabama, where there is more power available for them, but those centers need a lot of fiber to connect them back to a broader network, Miller says. Telecom companies are trying to get ahead of this: Zayo just bought Crown Castle's fiber segment and AT&T is reportedly in talks with Lumen Technologies to buy its fiber unit, following a deal for Lumen to provide fiber for Microsoft's data centers. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1300 ET - Rising demand for electricity in the U.S. and uncertainties about power-grid capacity increasingly are concerns not only for data-center developers but also for service providers catering to them, according to private-equity firms that back such businesses. For example, electricity sources were one of the main things Olympus Partners considered while studying an investment in Accelevation, a Miamisburg, Ohio-based provider of data-center design and installation services. "We spent a lot of time talking to their customers and making electricity-capacity analysis," says Rob Morris, Olympus's CEO. Eventually, Olympus got reassured that Accelevation's customers will be able to "manage [power supplies] in the places where they are putting their data centers," he says. Olympus bought the company in January. (luis.garcia@wsj.com; @lhvgarcia)

1238 ET - Cinema operators want to extend theatrical windows back toward 60 days as the number of habitual moviegoers slides, Benchmark analyst Mike Hickey says. Theater operators argue that the current 17- to 30-day movie-screening window is too short and undermines box office sales, as ticket revenue is down 24% from pre-Covid levels, Hickey says. North America has lost nearly 5,700 movie screens since the pandemic, and many theater chains are trying to integrate other forms of entertainment like bowling and arcade games to offset the slower slate of new film screenings. But to drive weekly attendance, volume consistency is more important than occasional blockbusters, Hickey says.(katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1111 ET - Novo Nordisk first-quarter earnings could miss expectations on lower-than-expected prescriptions of Ozempic and Wegovy as well as some price declines, Bank of America Securities analysts write. Growth will also be hit by tough comparables, currency impacts and possibly higher costs, the bank says. "We expect sales guide to be lowered from 16-24% at constant exchange rates to 14%-22%, with our forecasts at the bottom of the new range." BofA assumes limited Ozempic/Wegovy inflection in the second quarter, while a key unknown is if whether a second-half inflection in prescriptions happens against tougher comparables. The bank lowers its price objective to 910 Danish kroner from 1,075 kroner and keeps a buy rating. Shares trade at 475.00 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0457 ET - EssilorLuxottica should be able to post higher sales despite uncertainty in the U.S., RBC Capital Markets analysts say. The analysts expect the Franco-Italian eyewear group to report revenue of 6.94 billion euros, up 9.5% on year in reported terms. There are some concerns around the company's exposure to the U.S. market, which accounts for 45% of its revenue, according to RBC Capital Market estimates. "Despite increasing volatility in U.S. consumer market, we think EssilorLuxottica's momentum is unlikely to be materially affected," they say. The company has a stable underlying business and mid-term growth opportunities through innovation, the analysts add. Shares are down 0.4% at 268.30 euros. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0338 ET - Irish government bonds could stay weak relative to eurozone peers as the country's economy is exposed to tariff risks more than many others. "Among eurozone government bonds, we think tariff exposure is partly behind the underperformance of Irish credit this year given U.S. pharmaceutical companies' manufacturing there," Citi Research's Aman Bansal says in a note. Citi is long in Irish bonds but acknowledges that the tariff exposure might prevent a meaningful outperformance of the credit while the uncertainty lingers. "In contrast, Spain, Greece and France might be relatively shielded from U.S. reciprocal tariff impact due to their smaller export exposure," the rates strategist says. The 10-year Irish bond yield falls 3 basis point to 3.035%; the 10-year Bund yield falls 4.3bps to 2.734%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

2222 ET - IHH Healthcare's Turkish unit is likely to maintain its growth momentum despite political uncertainty there, Maybank Investment Bank analysts Nur Natasha Ariza and Yin Shao Yang say in a note. While accounting adjustments related to hyperinflation may weigh on reported earnings amid the Turkish lira's depreciation, core earnings should remain resilient, they say. The Acibadem unit benefits from foreign-currency-denominated medical tourism and steady private healthcare spending, they say. Contributing 30% of IHH's 2024 revenue, the Turkey business is projected to see 9% Ebitda growth in 2025, helped partly by stable in-patient volumes, they add. The analysts remain positive on Acibadem's non-lira income and IHH's other key segments. Maybank maintains a buy rating on IHH and keeps its target price at MYR7.97. Shares are unchanged at MYR6.91. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1823 ET - Pathology services provider Healius's target of a high-single-digit Ebit margin by FY 2027 looks ambitious to Jefferies. Healius expects to reach this goal through a mix of revenue and cost initiatives. However, analyst David Stanton forecasts Healius achieving a 4.4% margin in FY 2028. "Growth in Ebit margin is not expected to be linear in the timeframe--we believe it is likely to be back-ended," Jefferies says. "We estimate that low-end to high-end targets imply FY 2028 Pathology Ebit margins of 7.0% to 8.6%." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1812 ET - Pathology services provider Healius is in the recovery room, but Barrenjoey is skeptical that the company can reach performance targets laid out at its investor day yesterday. Healius wants to grow revenue at 6% and make a high-single-digit Ebit margin by FY 2027. Analyst Saul Hadassin says this implies Ebit growing at a compound annual rate of 120% from FY 2025-FY 2027. "While we anticipate an improvement in operating performance, this target looks difficult to deliver, particularly in light of looming cuts to VitB12/mid-stream urine tests," Barrenjoey says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1149 ET - Rio Tinto investor and investment-management company Ninety One is confident that the miner's board has done the necessary work to assess the merits of collapsing its dual-listed structure and trusts its view that it poses risks to shareholders, Portfolio Manager George Cheveley told Dow Jones Newswires. "We acknowledge that the structure is not ideal but believe Rio Tinto is at an exciting strategic point and want to see the management team focused on operational delivery," he added. U.K. hedge fund Palliser has tabled resolutions calling for an independent review into the miner's structure at its annual general meetings. Ninety One Ltd. has a $120 million stake in Rio Tinto PLC--or under 0.2% of its shares--according to data from Refinitiv. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1034 ET - The cost of insuring European bank bonds against default using credit default swaps rises due to increased concerns on the impact of tariffs ahead of the April 2 deadline, with many hitting their highest in 10 weeks or more. "European markets are on the back foot in early trade once again, with tariff fears driving risk assets lower globally," Scope Markets' Joshua Mahony says in a note. The five-year CDS on the U.K.'s HSBC 5-year CDS rises to its highest in more than five months at 55 basis points, while Germany's Deutsche Bank 5-year CDS trades at a 10-week high of 62 basis points, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. The 5-year CDS of UniCredit, NatWest, Santander and UBS also hit 10-week highs. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0956 ET - Stocks of European banks trade lower on Friday as the market awaits the rollout of Trump's tariff agenda on April 2. The Stoxx Europe 600 Banks index sheds 1.33% in afternoon trade, after falling as much as 1.92% in earlier exchanges with Germany's Commerzbank and Austria's Raiffeisen and Bawag seeing the steepest declines. "There is some apprehension around U.S. tariff announcements next week and a general sense of unease about what that may mean for the European economies," Keefe, Bruyette & Woods analyst Andrew Stimpson says. Shares in Deutsche Bank--which earlier announced the departure of its CFO and the renewal of its CEO's contract--also fall more than 2%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0617 ET - Irish bank shares have taken a hit as U.S. trade tariffs make headlines, given their potential impact on the country's economy, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note. The U.S. administration is eyeing the enviable budget surplus that Ireland has, partly thanks to large corporate tax receipts as well as the large trade surplus Ireland has with the U.S. in goods and pharmaceuticals in particular, analyst Andrew Stimpson writes. "We currently think it unlikely that there are widespread job losses from tariffs as Irish factories also serve the EU, not just the U.S.," he notes. AIB Group and Bank of Ireland have harsh downside scenarios in their provision forecasts, which include trade disruptions, and would take a hit of 10% and 20%, respectively, on profits, he adds. AIB is down 12% month-to-date while BoI has fallen 3%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0612 ET - Most large European banks recorded weaker liquidity ratios in the last quarter of 2024, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. Twenty of Europe's 30 largest banks by assets recorded declines in liquidity coverage ratios in the fourth quarter, S&P Global Market Intelligence says. Nine lenders reported increases, and one had an unchanged liquidity coverage ratio, it says. Liquidity coverage ratios measure banks' strength to cope with cash outflows for 30 days. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0554 ET - European banks are likely to issue the same or slightly fewer corporate bonds in 2025 relative to 2024, CreditSights analysts say in a note. Most European banks have met their minimum regulatory capital targets and therefore have no need to issue incremental debt, the analysts say. The level of new bond issuance can impact spread levels with higher issuance potentially causing credit spreads to widen, CreditSights says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0521 ET - Euro investment-grade credit spreads have tightened to nearly their tightest historical levels, boosting excess returns on the assets in the year to date, CreditSights analysts say in a note. On aggregate, the excess return gains on euro investment-grade bonds are plus 73 basis points year-to-date, comprised of 51 basis points from spread tightening and 22 basis points from carry, the analysts say. Some of the notable outperforming sectors are utilities, bank surbordinate bonds, insurance subordinate bonds, transportation and autos, CreditSights says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0432 ET - Deutsche Bank's CFO transition seems well organized and sets the stage for the next leg of the German group's strategy, which is expected to be announced along its 2025 results next year at the latest, J.P.Morgan says in a research note. DB announced the departure of its CFO James von Moltke when his contract expires in June 2026 and named Morgan Stanley's Raja Akram as his replacement. "The transition looks very well thought out with the designated CFO joining in October 2025 and overlapping with James," analysts Kian Abouhossein and Amit Ranjan write. They say the von Moltke's departure is a surprise as he and CEO Christian Sewing--whose contract was renewed until 2029--achieved a remarkable turnaround in the past five years. "The incoming CFO will have his work cut out," they note. Shares fall 2.5%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1513 ET - U.S. states and their pension plans are increasingly considering cryptocurrency as a reserve investment, S&P Global Ratings says. The recent creation of crypto exchange-traded funds and the evolving regulatory landscape may have increased interest in crypto by helping address associated risks, according to S&P. The agency also says 17 U.S. states either allow or have introduced legislation to allow crypto as a reserve asset, with 16 proceeding toward including crypto in statewide pension trusts. "Although crypto is still a very small allocation in U.S. state reserve andpension holdings, many states are in various stages of implementing policychanges to allow the use of bitcoin or other cryptocurrencies in their generalfund and/or pension trust assets," said S&P Global Ratings credit analyst Todd Kanaster. Earlier this month, President Trump said he would move forward on creating a U.S. crypto strategic reserve. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1020 ET - The strength in Canada's economy at the start of the year won't alter the central bank's view that it will need to balance the deflationary impact of the tariffs due to weaker economic activity against inflationary pressures arising from higher costs with the tariffs, Alberta Central's Charles St-Arnaud says. The economist says it is clear from recent comments by Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem that at this point there is more concern over inflationary risks, which suggests that without further meaningful tariffs being imposed in the coming weeks it is likely the BoC will keep its policy rate unchanged at the April meeting. Still, St-Arnaud said that while the bank may pause the general direction for interest rates is likely lower. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1011 ET - The Canadian economy's strong start to 2025 should keep the central bank on hold in April, strategists at TD Securities argue. Monthly GDP growth of 0.4% in January was ahead of expectations and, even with the flash estimate for growth to stall in February, leaves GDP for 1Q tracking near 2% annualized which matches the Bank of Canada's now dated projection from January, they say. The strategists note that growth will come under more pressure after tariffs take effect in March, but the latest data does leave the Canadian economy on a much stronger footing ahead of the trade shock. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1010 ET - The latest Canadian GDP numbers likely won't mean much for the Bank of Canada's next interest rate decision, reckons Oxford Economics' Michael Davenport, who continues to expect the central bank will hold steady as it tries to balance the negative hit to demand from tariffs against upside risks to inflation. The economist says the economy got an artificial boost in January as firms pulled forward exports to the U.S. ahead of tariffs, and temporary fiscal stimulus measures continued to support demand. "January may represent the peak in economic activity as the U.S.-Canada trade war pushes Canada's economy into a recession beginning in 2Q," he says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1008 ET - Solid Canadian GDP growth in January isn't a sign of things to come. The economy expanded a stronger-than-expected 0.4% on-month, and growth for December was revised up a tick. That, says Capital Economics' Thomas Ryan, shows the economy still had solid momentum to start the year. And the weak preliminary February estimate, showing GDP was flat, can't be blamed entirely o U.S. tariff threats since the winter weather was unseasonably harsh, he adds. Still, Ryan says it adds to the sense the economy is losing momentum, and that's before the full impact of the emerging trade war with the U.S. starts to show up in hard data and likely pushes Canada into a mild recession mid-year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0941 ET - Oil futures are lower but on track for a third straight weekly gain, with WTI hovering below the key $70 level. "Crude futures are still struggling to turn bullish," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. The coming seasonal ramp up in refineries to meet travel demand, and U.S. sanctionson Venezuelan and Iranian crude are supporting factors, but "the U.S. stock market is struggling, and longer-term demand fears are in the minds of most traders as tariffs begin to kick in on cars not manufactured in the U.S.," he says. WTI is off 0.2% at $69.77 a barrel, and Brent is 0.2% lower at $73.87 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0911 ET - Canada's economy looks to have flatlined last month after stronger-than-expected growth to start the year, a possible early sign tariff worries are weighing. Statistics Canada forecasts industry-level GDP was little changed in February following growth of 0.4% on-month in January, the strongest expansion since last April. Economists were expecting growth of 0.3% for January, in line with the data agency's earlier flash estimate. January's growth was buoyed largely by both goods-producing industries, with a further rise in mining and oil and gas extraction and a rebound in manufacturing. The biggest drag on growth came from a pullback in retail trade, which the early data shows continued into February. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0521 ET - Euro investment-grade credit spreads have tightened to nearly their tightest historical levels, boosting excess returns on the assets in the year to date, CreditSights analysts say in a note. On aggregate, the excess return gains on euro investment-grade bonds are plus 73 basis points year-to-date, comprised of 51 basis points from spread tightening and 22 basis points from carry, the analysts say. Some of the notable outperforming sectors are utilities, bank surbordinate bonds, insurance subordinate bonds, transportation and autos, CreditSights says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0432 ET - SSE isn't expected to undergo a major strategic review under its incoming CEO Martin Pibworth, Jefferies analysts say in a note. Pibworth is well known to investors and has a deep expertise in energy markets and the execution of large capital projects, Jefferies says. "Overall, we see his appointment as a clear positive for SSE," the analysts say. Shares in the U.K. energy group are up 1.5% at 1,570 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

2255 ET - Overseas oil fields will likely be the key production volume driver for Cnooc in 2025-2026 with projects in Brazil and Guyana, Citi analysts say in a research note. The analysts think Cnooc's 2024 reserve replacement ratio was "impressive" with 11 new discoveries and 30 successful appraisals. Management thinks China's natural gas prices should remain relatively more stable compared to the global market given that all natural gas produced by Cnooc is consumed domestically and its prices are not directly tied to international natural gas prices, the analysts note. Citi maintains a buy rating on Cnooc and keeps its target price at HK$24.00. Shares are last at 1.4% lower at HK$18.40. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2215 ET - RHB IB downgrades Malaysia's oil & gas sector to neutral from overweight, citing prolonged uncertainties in the industry. Brent crude may remain at $75/bbl in 2025-2026, with OPEC+ gradually increasing output from April, analyst Sean Lim says in a note. The oil market's deficit could narrow to 0.7mbpd in 2025 from 1.4mbpd in 2024, and may turn into a surplus by 2026 if production hikes continue, he reckons. Geopolitical risks are in focus, with regional tensions and U.S. sanctions affecting supply, he says. Domestically, Petronas' shift to half-yearly financial reporting from a quarterly basis, cost-cutting measures, and uncertainties surrounding capital expenditure add to the sector's challenges, he says. RHB favors upstream service providers with strong maintenance exposure and global diversification for more stable earnings. The brokerage names Bumi Armada, Yinson Holdings, and Dayang Enterprise as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2049 ET - Oil prices are mixed in early Asian trade. The oil market could see growing volatility and pullback risks, says Maria Agustina Patti, a financial markets strategist consultant at Exness. Prices could be supported by tighter global supply concerns, driven by U.S. tariff threats on Venezuelan oil buyers and ongoing sanctions on Iranian oil, Patti says in an email. Traders may stay cautious about any developments that may exacerbate supply disruptions, Patti adds. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are flat at $69.89/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are down 0.1% at $73.98/bbl.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

1520 ET - Fiber operations are a big opportunity for telecommunications companies to capitalize on the rise of data-center construction to power artificial intelligence, Citizens analyst Greg Miller tells WSJ. Data centers are increasingly being built in remote areas like North Dakota and Alabama, where there is more power available for them, but those centers need a lot of fiber to connect them back to a broader network, Miller says. Telecom companies are trying to get ahead of this: Zayo just bought Crown Castle's fiber segment and AT&T is reportedly in talks with Lumen Technologies to buy its fiber unit, following a deal for Lumen to provide fiber for Microsoft's data centers. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1149 ET - Rio Tinto investor and investment-management company Ninety One is confident that the miner's board has done the necessary work to assess the merits of collapsing its dual-listed structure and trusts its view that it poses risks to shareholders, Portfolio Manager George Cheveley told Dow Jones Newswires. "We acknowledge that the structure is not ideal but believe Rio Tinto is at an exciting strategic point and want to see the management team focused on operational delivery," he added. U.K. hedge fund Palliser has tabled resolutions calling for an independent review into the miner's structure at its annual general meetings. Ninety One Ltd. has a $120 million stake in Rio Tinto PLC--or under 0.2% of its shares--according to data from Refinitiv. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1033 ET - A hotter-than expected February core PCE reading sparks new concerns about stagflation, and the role tariffs may play going forward. "We are moving in the wrong direction and the concern is that tariffs threaten higher prices, which mean the inflation prints are going to remain hot," says ING Economics in a note. "This will constrain the Fed's ability to deliver further interest rate cuts." These concerns have pushed commodity futures into the red across the board, with the exception of precious metals and copper--with gold surging above the $3,100 per troy ounce threshold, and copper climbing back towards a record-high close. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1020 ET - The strength in Canada's economy at the start of the year won't alter the central bank's view that it will need to balance the deflationary impact of the tariffs due to weaker economic activity against inflationary pressures arising from higher costs with the tariffs, Alberta Central's Charles St-Arnaud says. The economist says it is clear from recent comments by Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem that at this point there is more concern over inflationary risks, which suggests that without further meaningful tariffs being imposed in the coming weeks it is likely the BoC will keep its policy rate unchanged at the April meeting. Still, St-Arnaud said that while the bank may pause the general direction for interest rates is likely lower. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1011 ET - The Canadian economy's strong start to 2025 should keep the central bank on hold in April, strategists at TD Securities argue. Monthly GDP growth of 0.4% in January was ahead of expectations and, even with the flash estimate for growth to stall in February, leaves GDP for 1Q tracking near 2% annualized which matches the Bank of Canada's now dated projection from January, they say. The strategists note that growth will come under more pressure after tariffs take effect in March, but the latest data does leave the Canadian economy on a much stronger footing ahead of the trade shock. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1010 ET - The latest Canadian GDP numbers likely won't mean much for the Bank of Canada's next interest rate decision, reckons Oxford Economics' Michael Davenport, who continues to expect the central bank will hold steady as it tries to balance the negative hit to demand from tariffs against upside risks to inflation. The economist says the economy got an artificial boost in January as firms pulled forward exports to the U.S. ahead of tariffs, and temporary fiscal stimulus measures continued to support demand. "January may represent the peak in economic activity as the U.S.-Canada trade war pushes Canada's economy into a recession beginning in 2Q," he says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1008 ET - Solid Canadian GDP growth in January isn't a sign of things to come. The economy expanded a stronger-than-expected 0.4% on-month, and growth for December was revised up a tick. That, says Capital Economics' Thomas Ryan, shows the economy still had solid momentum to start the year. And the weak preliminary February estimate, showing GDP was flat, can't be blamed entirely o U.S. tariff threats since the winter weather was unseasonably harsh, he adds. Still, Ryan says it adds to the sense the economy is losing momentum, and that's before the full impact of the emerging trade war with the U.S. starts to show up in hard data and likely pushes Canada into a mild recession mid-year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1005 ET - Economists are largely treating Canada's strong GDP increase in January with a shrug. The data, they say, is old news, given President Trump's tariffs to date on Canada aluminum and steel, and more to come next week, highlighted by a 25% levy on automobiles and auto parts. BMO Capital Markets chief economist Doug Porter says January's 0.4% month-over-month increase, and a flattening in February, puts 1Q GDP on the path toward 1.5%-to-2% annualized advance, or largely aligned with Bank of Canada expectations. "The rollicking start to the year is but cold comfort with tariffs now in place and poised to ramp up." Porter says the data show Canada's economy was solid ahead of the escalation in the trade conflict, and should reinforce BOC's "newfound lean to the sidelines" in April. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0911 ET - Canada's economy looks to have flatlined last month after stronger-than-expected growth to start the year, a possible early sign tariff worries are weighing. Statistics Canada forecasts industry-level GDP was little changed in February following growth of 0.4% on-month in January, the strongest expansion since last April. Economists were expecting growth of 0.3% for January, in line with the data agency's earlier flash estimate. January's growth was buoyed largely by both goods-producing industries, with a further rise in mining and oil and gas extraction and a rebound in manufacturing. The biggest drag on growth came from a pullback in retail trade, which the early data shows continued into February. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0907 ET - Canadian aluminum and steel producers, in the spotlight after the Trump administration slapped 25% tariffs on imports of the metals, rely heavily on trade between the countries. Exports to the U.S. accounted for 16.8% of Canadian GDP and more than 2.6 million Canadian jobs in 2023, the most recent year where value-added data is available. Statistics Canada notes alumina and aluminum production and processing industry had 68% of output attributed to direct exports to the U.S. Canada's steel industry and iron and steel mills and ferro-alloy manufacturing industry were also highly reliant on exports to the U.S., with 69% and 66% of their output attributed to U.S. demand, respectively. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

2230 ET - China Sunsine Chemical's sales volumes are likely to sustain an uptrend, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a research report, as they raise its target price for the stock to S$0.63 from S$0.58, maintaining a buy rating. The rubber chemicals producer's sales in China are expected to improve, supported by the country's targeted stimulus measures to aid consumption recovery, the analysts say. Also, according to Tireworld, China's tire industry data service provider, the nation's rubber tire exports grew 3.3% on year in January-February 2025, which suggests continued growth in international sales volume for the company, as China Sunsine Chemical's products are used for tire production, they say. Shares are 0.9% higher at S$0.545. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1512 ET - Chemours, the producer of fluorine-based refrigerants, specialty plastics and titanium dioxide pigments, is entering a more favorable environment, according to Mizuho in a research report. Analysts say one major tailwind is that industry stockpiling of the banned refrigerant Freon is normalizing amid the larger shift to next-gen refrigerants, of which Chemours is one of only two producers. The analysts also say that litigation surrounding PFAS, also known as forever chemicals, seems to be moving in the right direction. They believe a potential settlement in advance of the New Jersey PFAS trial, anticipated in spring/summer, would likely help limit the scope of Chemours' potential liability. Mizuho upgrades Chemours to outperform from neutral. Chemours is up 2.6% to $14.19. (sarina.isaacs@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1601 GMT - Credit investor focus will be on U.S. tariffs announcements due on Wednesday, Societe Generale's Juan Valencia says in a note. Markets are more cautious after President Trump announced 25% tariffs on all auto imports into the U.S. An aggressive stance involving blanket tariffs across countries is likely to drive credit spreads volatility, Valencia says. "An announcement in line with market expectations or a little better should see credit spreads improve over the coming weeks," he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1411 GMT - The Canadian economy's strong start to 2025 should keep the central bank on hold in April, strategists at TD Securities argue. Monthly GDP growth of 0.4% in January was ahead of expectations and, even with the flash estimate for growth to stall in February, leaves GDP for 1Q tracking near 2% annualized which matches the Bank of Canada's now dated projection from January, they say. The strategists note that growth will come under more pressure after tariffs take effect in March, but the latest data does leave the Canadian economy on a much stronger footing ahead of the trade shock. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1410 GMT - The latest Canadian GDP numbers likely won't mean much for the Bank of Canada's next interest rate decision, reckons Oxford Economics' Michael Davenport, who continues to expect the central bank will hold steady as it tries to balance the negative hit to demand from tariffs against upside risks to inflation. The economist says the economy got an artificial boost in January as firms pulled forward exports to the U.S. ahead of tariffs, and temporary fiscal stimulus measures continued to support demand. "January may represent the peak in economic activity as the U.S.-Canada trade war pushes Canada's economy into a recession beginning in 2Q," he says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1408 GMT - Solid Canadian GDP growth in January isn't a sign of things to come. The economy expanded a stronger-than-expected 0.4% on-month, and growth for December was revised up a tick. That, says Capital Economics' Thomas Ryan, shows the economy still had solid momentum to start the year. And the weak preliminary February estimate, showing GDP was flat, can't be blamed entirely o U.S. tariff threats since the winter weather was unseasonably harsh, he adds. Still, Ryan says it adds to the sense the economy is losing momentum, and that's before the full impact of the emerging trade war with the U.S. starts to show up in hard data and likely pushes Canada into a mild recession mid-year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1405 GMT - Economists are largely treating Canada's strong GDP increase in January with a shrug. The data, they say, is old news, given President Trump's tariffs to date on Canada aluminum and steel, and more to come next week, highlighted by a 25% levy on automobiles and auto parts. BMO Capital Markets chief economist Doug Porter says January's 0.4% month-over-month increase, and a flattening in February, puts 1Q GDP on the path toward 1.5%-to-2% annualized advance, or largely aligned with Bank of Canada expectations. "The rollicking start to the year is but cold comfort with tariffs now in place and poised to ramp up." Porter says the data show Canada's economy was solid ahead of the escalation in the trade conflict, and should reinforce BOC's "newfound lean to the sidelines" in April. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1336 GMT - The winner from President Trump's plans to impose 25% tariffs on imported vehicles? No one, say analysts at Wedbush in a research note. While Trump is pushing for more auto manufacturing in the US, the analysts note that even US automakers that produce domestically have around half of their auto parts come from foreign markets. Tesla, for example, will be affected, as CEO Elon Musk himself acknowledged on his social media platform. "A US car with all US parts made in the US is a fictional tale not even possible today," say the analysts. They believe they will add up to $100 billion of costs annually for the auto industry, something that will be passed to the consumer and erode demand. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1331 GMT - Germany's labor market cooled further in March, and the situation could get worse, Deutsche Bank Research economist Marc Schattenberg says in a note. Seasonally adjusted unemployment ticked up to 6.3% from 6.2% in February, while the number of unemployed rose surprisingly sharply by around 26,000 to 2.92 million, he says. Job losses could persist, especially in the manufacturing sector, not least from the repercussions of U.S. trade policy on key German industries, Schattenberg says. Early labor market indicators don't point to any improvement in the coming months, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1329 GMT - Germany's labor market continued its gradual weakening in March as recruitment plans continue to shrink, ING's Carsten Brzeski says in a note. Seasonally adjusted unemployment rose by 26,000, increasing the unemployment rate to 6.3% from 6.2% in February. The unadjusted unemployment number of 2.967 million--albeit a fall from February--is the lowest March figure since the financial crisis, he says. Continued announcements of potential cost-cutting measures in the automotive and other industries are set to push up unemployment further, he says. The latest GfK consumer-confidence reading also published Friday suggests German consumers are focusing more on the cooling labor market than on politics even after elections brought added certainty, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1327 GMT - Michelin volumes remain subdued, but EPS is supported by pricing, cost discipline, buybacks, and low financial costs, HSBC analysts write. Following Michelin's February tire market volume data, the bank reduces its 2025 volume estimate by 1%. It expects the first quarter to be the weakest quarter due to tough comparables for light-vehicle tires and still-low demand for truck, construction, and mining tires. Nonetheless, HSBC models a progressive improvement throughout the year. "In our view, continued strength in free cash flow should support higher shareholder returns and be a positive catalyst for the stock." HSBC upgrades the stock to buy from hold and increases its target price to 38 euros from 36 euros. Shares rise 0.6% to 33.04 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1253 GMT - Ferrari has presented a clear and swift response to the 25% U.S. auto tariff hike, shouldering some of the burden while also making some price increases, Jefferies analyst James Grzinic writes. The bank estimates Ferrari's direct profit hit this year to be rather immaterial, at around 35 million euros, though the extent to which the balance of the pain will be divided up between dealers and clients is still unclear. "Perhaps the main take-away here is the group's unique ability to provide a very explicit response to such a major, and swift, impact on cost of goods sold." The fact that full-year earnings guidance was reiterated confirms the extent to which contingencies had been built into the outlook, Jefferies adds. Shares rise 3.8%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1041 GMT - Auto component manufacturer ZF is hoping for a quick agreement on U.S tariffs to avoid a new wave of protectionism that would affect the German company and have a negative global impact on growth, prosperity and jobs. ZF is investing $500 million in the expansion of its production plant in South Carolina as part of plans to bring more manufacturing closer to its customers and respond faster to changes in demand. "Our approach of producing as many products as possible locally in the respective markets proves to be correct and necessary," a ZF spokesperson said. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1027 GMT - The cost of insuring euro junk bonds against default using credit default swaps rises to a 10-week high as market sentiment deteriorates due to intensifying global trade tensions. The U.S. announcement of 25% tariffs on all auto imports has renewed concerns about global trade disputes, weakening appetite for risk assets, ActivTrades' Ricardo Evangelista says in a note. The iTraxx Europe Crossover index which tracks euro junk bond credit default swaps rises 2 basis points to 318bps, a level last reached in mid-January, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

RNS Number : 7646C STS Global Income & Growth Trust 28 March 2025  

STS GLOBAL INCOME & GROWTH TRUST PLC

LEI: 549300UZ1Y7PPQYJGE19

TRANSACTION IN OWN SHARES

STS Global Income & Growth Trust plc (the "Company") today purchased 50,000 of its own Ordinary shares at a price of 240.50 pence per share, to be held in Treasury.

Following the transaction, the Company's share capital comprises:

121,211,415 Issued Ordinary Shares (excluding Treasury shares)

53,976,770 Ordinary shares held in Treasury

175,188,185 Issued Ordinary Shares (including Treasury shares)

For reporting purposes under the FCA's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules the market should exclude any Ordinary shares held in Treasury and should use the figure 121,211,415 when determining if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest, in the Company.

Juniper Partners Limited

Company Secretary

Enquiries: 0131 378 0500

This information is provided by RNS, the news service of the London Stock Exchange. RNS is approved by the Financial Conduct Authority to act as a Primary Information Provider in the United Kingdom. Terms and conditions relating to the use and distribution of this information may apply. For further information, please contact rns@lseg.com or visit www.rns.com.

RNS may use your IP address to confirm compliance with the terms and conditions, to analyse how you engage with the information contained in this communication, and to share such analysis on an anonymised basis with others as part of our commercial services. For further information about how RNS and the London Stock Exchange use the personal data you provide us, please see our Privacy Policy.  END  POSQVLFLEXLLBBB

data.title: Update: UK CMA Weighs Airline Commitments to Address Transatlantic Competition Concerns
data.description:
(Updates with replies from International Airlines Group and American Airlines in seventh and eighth paragraphs)

The UK's Competition and Markets Authority is consulting on whether to accept commitments from five airlines to resolve competition concerns over their transatlantic cooperation, the regulator said Friday.

British Airways, Iberia, Aer Lingus, American Airlines , and Finnair coordinate on UK-US flights under the Atlantic Joint Business Agreement.

The proposed commitments would require American Airlines and British Airways, the only two AJBA airlines operating direct flights on certain routes, to offer flight slots at Heathrow and Gatwick on three key UK to US routes: London to Boston, London to Miami, and London to Chicago.

The commitments would also require them to carry a minimum number of local passengers on the London to Dallas route each year and provide competitors with access to connecting passengers on preferential terms.

Slots at Heathrow and Gatwick are difficult to obtain as airlines retain them if they meet annual usage requirements, making them rarely available. The commitments aim to reduce this barrier, allowing new competition on the affected routes.

The CMA began investigating the AJBA in 2018 as prior commitments imposed by the European Commission neared expiration. Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the CMA extended key terms for five years. With the airline sector recovered, the regulator is now reviewing the new commitments before making a final decision.

"We have been working constructively with the Competition and Markets Authority throughout this process and we await the completion of the market testing," International Airlines Group, which owns British Airways, Iberia, and Aer Lingus, told MT Newswires.

American Airlines also assured full cooperation on the review of the AJB, adding the agreement has benefited travelers with cheaper fares and more travel options.

Finnair did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' request for comment.

data.title: Workiva Is Maintained at Buy by Goldman Sachs
data.description: (16:15 GMT) Workiva Price Target Cut to $118.00/Share From $132.00 by Goldman Sachs

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1214 ET - Data tracking dryness in U.S. crop-growing areas is gaining importance with analysts and traders, with extreme drought spreading in the Northern Plains and in Texas, according to data from the U.S. Drought Monitor issued Thursday. While the data show expanded areas of drought across the Corn Belt, key rainfall is expected to hit the Eastern Corn Belt soon. "7-Day precipitation accumulation has been boosted to 2-4" east of the Mississippi River," says Daniel Flynn of Price Futures Group in a note. "This matches what's needed to ease/end drought there, and so is viewed as beneficial." Rainfall totals are becoming increasingly important as the spring planting season nears its start in April. CBOT corn is down 0.2%, soybeans are flat, and wheat slides 1.4%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1149 ET - Rio Tinto investor and investment-management company Ninety One is confident that the miner's board has done the necessary work to assess the merits of collapsing its dual-listed structure and trusts its view that it poses risks to shareholders, Portfolio Manager George Cheveley told Dow Jones Newswires. "We acknowledge that the structure is not ideal but believe Rio Tinto is at an exciting strategic point and want to see the management team focused on operational delivery," he added. U.K. hedge fund Palliser has tabled resolutions calling for an independent review into the miner's structure at its annual general meetings. Ninety One Ltd. has a $120 million stake in Rio Tinto PLC--or under 0.2% of its shares--according to data from Refinitiv. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1140 ET - Lean hog futures on the CME are up 0.3% in morning trade, staying up for the day despite the negative momentum on the commodities space in general. That's because the Hogs and Pigs report released by the USDA Thursday showed slightly lower inventories than expected. "Friendly report versus expectations," says Ben Johnson of New Frontier Capital Markets. "Supply expectations for the summer have been lowered." The focus is now on April 2 'reciprocal' tariffs, which is injecting uncertainty into markets after the PCE report from the Commerce Department showed signs of resurgent stagflation. Live cattle is flat today. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1121 ET - The premium of copper's Comex contract over the LME is likely to endure, with tariff-related disruptions dominating market sentiment, according to Capital Economics. A U.S. Commerce Department report on copper tariffs is due in November, but an announcement could come much sooner. "Following the imposition of 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum imports in mid-March, traders have understandably been acting as if the outcome of the copper probe is a done-deal," says David Oxley, chief climate and commodities economist. The U.S. imports about 50% of the copper it consumes, and domestic copper producers are unlikely to be able to ramp up production quickly enough to offset any shortfall. "Despite rising to a record high in absolute terms, the fact that the copper price on the Comex is still 'only' 15% higher than that on the LME means that the spread could potentially widen further," Oxley says. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1033 ET - A hotter-than expected February core PCE reading sparks new concerns about stagflation, and the role tariffs may play going forward. "We are moving in the wrong direction and the concern is that tariffs threaten higher prices, which mean the inflation prints are going to remain hot," says ING Economics in a note. "This will constrain the Fed's ability to deliver further interest rate cuts." These concerns have pushed commodity futures into the red across the board, with the exception of precious metals and copper--with gold surging above the $3,100 per troy ounce threshold, and copper climbing back towards a record-high close. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1016 ET - Tariff concerns are hitting grains, with most-active CBOT corn down 1.5%, soybeans off 0.6%, and wheat sinking 2.3%. Worries about how reciprocal tariffs expected on April 2 will affect commodities demand is weighing on the agricultural complex. "The uncertainty of it all has fear as the dominant factor in the markets heading into the weekend," says Arlan Suderman of StoneX in a note. In particular, Suderman is watching for retaliatory tariffs from Mexico, which would have a big effect on U.S. grain exports. "Retaliatory tariffs by Mexico on Ag commodities could crush demand for vital commodities," he says. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1005 ET - Economists are largely treating Canada's strong GDP increase in January with a shrug. The data, they say, is old news, given President Trump's tariffs to date on Canada aluminum and steel, and more to come next week, highlighted by a 25% levy on automobiles and auto parts. BMO Capital Markets chief economist Doug Porter says January's 0.4% month-over-month increase, and a flattening in February, puts 1Q GDP on the path toward 1.5%-to-2% annualized advance, or largely aligned with Bank of Canada expectations. "The rollicking start to the year is but cold comfort with tariffs now in place and poised to ramp up." Porter says the data show Canada's economy was solid ahead of the escalation in the trade conflict, and should reinforce BOC's "newfound lean to the sidelines" in April. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1002 ET - Dutch Bros plans to double its footprint with 1,000 new shops over the next five years, executives say at the company's inaugural investor day. The coffee chain has also increased its total addressable market to 7,000 locations, up from its previous target of 4,000 and the 6,000-shop estimate of William Blair analysts. Those analysts say the key to the company's growth will be scaling its culture and leadership pipeline. The brand's strong service levels are a meaningful differentiating factor and contribute to its standout value scores that beat its peers and fast-food players like McDonald's and Chick-fil-A, the analysts say in a research note. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0957 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are lower as the May contract takes the front month position following a bigger-than-expected storage build that narrowed inventory deficits and confirmed an early start to the injection season. "Four of the past five EIA reports were bearish--suggesting a market struggling to pinpoint supply/demand balances--as storage deficits are evaporating rapidly," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. "The fundamental outlook into next winter is bullish, but soft spring gas prices may be the most-likely scenario first." The front month is off 0.5% at $3.905/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0946 ET - Gold prices soar to a fresh record high in afternoon trade, reaching a peak of $3,124.40 a troy ounce as concerns over global trade tensions and an uncertain economic outlook spur safe-haven demand. Futures now trade 0.7% higher at $3,111.80 a troy ounce and are up nearly 3% on the week. "The gold rally is not quite over yet," says Barbara Lambrecht, commodity analyst at Commerzbank Research. "We consider gold to be well supported for the time being, especially as next week's potentially disappointing U.S. economic indicators could strengthen hopes of more interest rate cuts by the Fed." Meanwhile, the latest U.S. data showed the PCE price index--the Federal Reserve's preferred gauge of inflation--rose 2.5% in the 12 months through February, still above the central bank's 2% target. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0941 ET - Oil futures are lower but on track for a third straight weekly gain, with WTI hovering below the key $70 level. "Crude futures are still struggling to turn bullish," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. The coming seasonal ramp up in refineries to meet travel demand, and U.S. sanctionson Venezuelan and Iranian crude are supporting factors, but "the U.S. stock market is struggling, and longer-term demand fears are in the minds of most traders as tariffs begin to kick in on cars not manufactured in the U.S.," he says. WTI is off 0.2% at $69.77 a barrel, and Brent is 0.2% lower at $73.87 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0907 ET - Canadian aluminum and steel producers, in the spotlight after the Trump administration slapped 25% tariffs on imports of the metals, rely heavily on trade between the countries. Exports to the U.S. accounted for 16.8% of Canadian GDP and more than 2.6 million Canadian jobs in 2023, the most recent year where value-added data is available. Statistics Canada notes alumina and aluminum production and processing industry had 68% of output attributed to direct exports to the U.S. Canada's steel industry and iron and steel mills and ferro-alloy manufacturing industry were also highly reliant on exports to the U.S., with 69% and 66% of their output attributed to U.S. demand, respectively. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: Lottery.com Wins Dismissals of Three Lawsuits in Texas Court
data.description:
Lottery.com said the US District Court for the Western District of Texas granted motions to dismiss three lawsuits against the company for failure to state a claim

The suits were dismissed with prejudice and barred plaintiffs from refiling the same claims, Lottery.com said Friday in a statement.

The court denied all pending motions as moot and directed the court clerk to close all the cases, the company said.

Lottery.com shares rose 3.1% in recent Friday trading, paring earlier gains.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson unit ordered to pay $1.64 billion in HIV drug marketing case
data.description:
Key points:
  • Janssen was sued over Prezista, Intelence marketing
  • Award followed June 2024 jury verdict
  • Johnson & Johnson not immediately available for comment

A federal judge ordered a Johnson & Johnson unit on Friday to pay the U.S. government $1.64 billion after a jury found it liable in a whistleblower lawsuit for illegally promoting the HIV drugs Prezista and Intelence.

U.S. District Judge Zahid Quraishi in Trenton, New Jersey, ordered the drugmaker's Janssen unit to pay $360 million for violating the federal False Claims Act.

He also imposed $1.28 billion in civil fines, or $8,000 for each of 159,574 false claims the jury found were submitted to programs such as Medicare, Medicaid and the AIDS Drug Assistance Program.

Quraishi set aside the jury's $30 million award for violations of various state false claims laws, citing a lack of evidence. The June 13, 2024 verdict followed a six-week trial.

Janssen had sought a new trial, saying the verdict was tainted by a lack of proof and by erroneous jury instructions.

Neither Johnson & Johnson nor Janssen's lawyers immediately responded to requests for comment. Johnson & Johnson is based in New Brunswick, New Jersey.

The plaintiffs Jessica Penelow and Christine Brancaccio, who worked as Janssen sales representatives, accused Janssen of improperly marketing Prezista and Intelence for off-label uses.

This included promoting Prezista as "lipid-neutral," meaning it would not affect cholesterol or triglyceride levels, contrary to its U.S. Food and Drug Administration-approved label.

The plaintiffs also said Janssen paid doctors to promote the drugs at dinner and speaker programs, with the payments amounting to kickbacks.

Jurors found Janssen liable for some off-label claims, and no liability for the kickback claims.

In a 35-page decision, Quraishi said there was sufficient evidence that Janssen's off-label marketing was a "substantial factor in causing physicians to submit claims for reimbursement to government payors, and that it was reasonably foreseeable that false claims would result from such conduct."

The $360 million award is three times the $120 million the jury awarded under the federal False Claims Act, which allows triple damages.

False claims statutes let whistleblowers sue on behalf of governments, and share in recoveries.

A lawyer for the plaintiffs had no immediate comment.

The case is U.S. ex rel Penelow et al v Janssen Products LP, U.S. District Court, District of New Jersey, No. 12-07758.

data.title: Croatian share indices decline, ZABA in spotlight
data.description:
ZAGREB (Croatia), March 28 (SeeNews) - Losses in Hrvatski Telekom [ZSE:HT] and Valamar Rivera [ZSE:RIVP] pushed lower the main equity indices of the Zagreb Stock Exchange, bourse data showed on Friday.

The 18-share equity benchmark, the CROBEX, fell 0.11% to 3,329.27 points.

The narrower CROBEX10 index shed 0.33% to 2,090.04 points.

In regular trading, Zagrebacka Banka (ZABA) [ZSE:ZABA] generated the biggest daily turnover, 325,000 euro ($352,000), 0as its shares gained 0.73% to 27.70 euro. ZABA is part of the wider index.

Index heavyweight Hrvatski Telekom declined 0.69% to 43.40 euro in a turnover of some 307,000 euro.

Hotel owner and manager Valamar Rivera lost 0.35% to 5.70 euro in a turnover of 133,000 euro.

The bourse's regular turnover reached 1.4 million euro from 1.1 million euro a day earlier.

Besides it, the bourse registered three block deals with a total value of 1.3 million euro. In one of them, 25,000 shares of ZABA changed hands at a price of 27.50 euro apiece. In the second one, 700 shares of electrical concern Koncar [ZSE:KOEI] traded at a price of 470 euro per share. In the third block deal, a total of 8,700 shares of hotel owner and manager Arena Hospitality [ZSE:ARNT] were sold at a price of 35 euro per share.

($ = 0.924 euro)

data.title: Week Ahead - March 31st
data.description:
Next week, investors will closely monitor developments in the trade war, as reciprocal tariffs on imports to the US are set to take effect on April 2nd, including a 25% levy on autos.

The US jobs report will also capture attention, alongside the ISM PMIs, which will provide insights into the labour market and private sector performance.

Additionally, the official and Caixin PMIs for China, Japan's Tankan business survey, preliminary inflation figures for the Eurozone, and Germany's factory orders will be in focus.

The PMIs for Europe's largest economies will also be closely watched.

Meanwhile, the Reserve Bank of Australia is expected to keep interest rates unchanged.
